text: Robotic Assisted Hiatal Hernia Repair After Esophagectomy, presented by Fazaldeen Mogul in conjunction with Dr. Abu Bakr Ali at Sinai Grace Hospital. The patient is a fifty-seven year old male with a history of dysphagia that progressed from solids to liquids. Multiple CAT scans and endoscopies with biopsies revealed a six centimeter benign distal esophageal stricture. The patient failed conservative therapy consisting of repeated esophageal dilations. He became increasingly malnourished resulting in placement of a feeding jejunostomy tube. The patient underwent a hybrid Ivor Lewis esophagectomy with a complete laparoscopic abdominal portion and an open right thoracotomy. During the esophagectomy, the stomach was not tubularized due to the stomach already having a narrow configuration and the right gastric artery was preserved. Here we note the incision of the right cruce during the esophagectomy.

The patient is positioned in the modified French position with dual lumen intubation. A varus needle is inserted in the right upper quadrant to insufflate the abdomen, avoiding the jejunostomy tube on the left. The falciform ligament is taken down, and a Nathanson retractor is inserted. Laparoscopic instruments are used to carefully reduce the herniated small bowel, revealing the majority of the small bowel within the hernia sac. Further reduction exposes the transverse colon within the hernia sac. The hernia sac and associated adhesions are transected to visualize the left and right diaphragmatic crura.

The surgeon assesses blood perfusion to the stomach using near-infrared technology. The right upper quadrant camera port is exchanged from a 5mm to an 8mm port, and the XI robot platform is docked. Abdominal pressure is decreased to facilitate primary hernia repair using interrupted sutures. The repair is performed from anterior to posterior to avoid manipulating the gastric conduit. The right cruce, noted to be weak from previous incision during esophagectomy, is included in the cruroplasty without mesh reinforcement.

The incidence of diaphragmatic hernia after esophagectomy ranges from point six percent to nine percent, with increased incidence with a minimally invasive approach. The recommendation is to repair the hernia, especially when symptomatic, to prevent strangulation. Prophylactic choroplasty during esophagectomy has not been shown to decrease the incidence of the hernia. The higher incidence of hiatal hernia after minimally invasive esophagectomy may be related to increased adhesions in the open approach, preventing herniation. We found the robotic approach to cruroplasty facilitated adequate evaluation and closure of the defect.
